segments.s337-g49: This is a poetic noun-list introduced by the preposition به (here 'for'/'in respect of'). Each noun pair is linked by و 'and'; there is no finite verb in this phrase — the nouns are presented as items or qualities. مردی is an abstract noun meaning 'manliness/courage', بخشش functions as 'generosity/donation', داد is a noun 'justice' (not a past tense verb), and هنر here means 'excellence/virtue' rather than a literal fine art.
segments.s337-g50: Another coordinated list introduced by به ('for'). The nouns are enumerated with و 'and' between them; the sequence groups outward appearance/rank and regal attributes. دیدار here functions as a poetic nominative (sight/visage/meeting), بالا means 'high/lofty' (rank), فر is the technical epic term for 'royal glory', and گهر means 'gem/jewel'.
segments.s337-g51: The phrase begins with به رزم 'in/for battle' (به marking the circumstance), then اندرون 'within', followed by the noun پیل 'elephant' and the older/poetic form بد which here functions as an archaic past/copular 'was' (equivalent to بود). The final با سپاه 'with the host' is an adjunct. Word order is circumstantial phrase → locative → subject → verb → adjunct, typical of poetic inversion.
segments.s337-g52: This mirrors the previous half-line: به بزم 'in the banquet' sets the scene, اندرون 'within', ماه is used metaphorically for 'the beautiful one' (a beloved/beauty), followed by the archaic copula بد = 'was', and با کلاه 'with a cap/hat' as the accompanying detail. The segment is parallel structure to the war-line and uses ماه figuratively.
segments.s337-g9: شما (you) precedes the verb for emphasis; بازگردید is a 2pl imperative/optative form 'return/go back' addressed to 'شما'. ز + noun (ز این ریگ خشک) marks origin: 'from this dry sand', with the adjective خشک following the noun as in classical Persian ('ریگِ خشک' = 'dry sand').
segments.s337-g10: مباشید is the negative imperative/prohibitive form 'do not be/remain' (2pl). اگر introduces the conditional clause 'if …'. بارد here functions as an old/poetic present/subjunctive of باریدن 'to fall/rain' (i.e. 'if it should rain'); از ابر مشک is the prepositional phrase 'from a musk-cloud' describing the source of the falling.
segments.s337-g11: ز کوه اندر آید is a poetic locative phrase 'out of/from the mountain comes' (آید = 'comes', present/narrative). یکی is an indefinite 'a certain/one', and باد سخت is the noun + adjective 'a severe/violent wind' (سخت = 'harsh, severe').
segments.s337-g12: کجا is the interrogative 'where'. بشکند is the subjunctive/optative form of شکستن with the poetic prefix بـ- and here means 'will break / would break'. شاخ و برگ درخت is the object 'the tree's branches and leaves' with the noun درخت following its parts.
segments.s337-g13: ببارد is a classical present/subjunctive form 'will fall/it falls' (here of precipitation). بسی is an adverb meaning 'much' or 'greatly'. برف ز ابر سیاه = 'snow from a black/dark cloud' (ز = 'from').
segments.s337-g14: شما 'you' is the addressee; سوی ایران 'toward Iran' is the directional phrase. نیابید is the negative form of یابیدن used in classical Persian to indicate 'will not find' / 'cannot find' (final verb governs the clause). راه is the object 'way/path'.
segments.s337-g15: سر مهتران literally 'the heads of the chiefs' (i.e., their spirits/minds). زآن 'from/at that' links to the previous mention; سخن شد گران is a predicate phrase 'the speech/news became heavy/grievous' with شد (to become) following the noun and the adjective گران after it—a common classical order.
segments.s337-g16: بخفتند is past plural 'they slept/they lay down' (classical past with ب-). با درد means 'with pain/sorrow' (dرد here is emotional sorrow), and کنداواران is the plural subject 'the people/inhabitants of Kandavar' placed after the verb—typical flexible word order in poetry.
segments.s337-g33: This is two coordinated nominal descriptions with a copula for the first clause: زمین (subject) + گرم و نرم (adjectives) + است (copula ‘is’). The second coordinated part, و روشن هوا, omits the copula and places the adjective روشن before the noun هوا for a succinct, poetic order meaning ‘and the air is clear.’
segments.s337-g34: بدین is the contracted/prepositional sense (ب + این) meaning ‘in/with this’; رنجگی is an archaic noun meaning a state of suffering/fatigue; نیست + ... روا is a negated impersonal judgment: رفتن (infinitive) is the grammatical subject of the predicative روا (‘fitting/permissible’), so the clause says ‘to go is not fitting in this state.’
segments.s337-g35: بر آن چشمه marks location (‘at/on that spring’), یکسر is an adverb ‘all at once/entirely,’ and فرود آمدند is the past plural verb ‘they descended/ alighted/ came down.’ Word order is subject‑omitted; the place phrase leads and the verb finishes the clause.
segments.s337-g36: ز here is the poetic preposition introducing the indirect object/beneficiary (to/about Khosrow); بسی modifies داستانها (‘many/much of tales’), and زدند is used idiomatically with داستانها — in classical Persian داستان زدن means ‘to tell/recite tales,’ so زدند functions as ‘they told/recited.’
segments.s337-g37: که is the subordinating conjunction ‘that’; چونین is an emphatic demonstrative ‘such’; شگفتی is the noun ‘marvel/wonder’; نبیند is the negative present/subjunctive form ‘does not/has not seen’ (here reporting that nobody has witnessed it); کسی with the negative verb yields ‘no one.’ The clause therefore means ‘that no one has seen such a marvel.’
segments.s337-g38: وگر is ‘and if’/’but if’; در زمانه is a locative phrase ‘in the age/world’; بماند is the present/subjunctive of ‘remain’ (‘should remain’ or ‘were to remain’); بسی here functions adverbially to indicate extent/duration (‘for long’). The clause gives a hypothetical continuation: ‘and if one were to remain long in the age…’
segments.s337-g39: کز is a compound/poetic conjunction meaning ‘that/for which/from which’; این رفتن شاه is a noun-phrase ‘this going/departure of the king’; نادیده‌ایم is a perfect-form used idiomatically to mean ‘we have not seen/there is no precedent in our experience.’ Together: ‘we have not seen a king depart thus.’
segments.s337-g40: ز here means ‘from’; گردنکشان (‘those who wrench necks’) is a classical term for ‘rebels’ or ‘insurgents’; نیز ‘also/too’; نشنیده‌ایم is the negative perfect ‘we have not heard (of it).’ The clause says they have not even heard of such a thing from the rebels.
segments.s337-g25: خروشان (an adverbial adjective) modifies the following verb phrase and means 'roaringly / in a surging manner'. بدان is the contracted classical form of به آن ('to that'), here split in the tokens as ب + دان; بازآمدند is the simple past plural 'they returned / came back'. Word order is slightly poetic (adverb + destination + verb) and the implicit subject 'they' is carried by the verb ending.
segments.s337-g26: پر از ... آمدند is a predicate phrase: پر از 'full of' + noun phrase غم دل 'sorrow of heart'; با گداز is an instrumental/attendant phrase 'with (a) melting / burning (pain)', where گداز is a poetic noun for ardor/anguish; آمدند repeats the past plural 'they came', reinforcing the same subject. The components are linked coordinatively (they came full of X and with Y).
segments.s337-g27: بر آن آب = 'upon/on that water' (preposition + demonstrative + noun). هر کس که آمد is a relative/indefinite clause 'whoever came'; فرود (literally 'descent' or 'alighting') here completes the idea 'came down' — the verb آمد precedes the noun فرود in a poetic inversion (normally فرود آمد).
segments.s337-g28: همی داد is a classical habitual/continuous past construction where همی indicates ongoing or customary action and داد is 'gave' (here 'would give' or 'offered'). شاه جهان را درود is the object: درود 'greeting' directed to شاه جهان 'the king of the world', with را marking the definite object. The subject is the indefinite 'هر کس' carried from the previous segment.
segments.s337-g29: فریبرز گفت is straight narrative past 'Fariburz said'. آنچه introduces a content clause 'that which / what', and خسرو بگفت reports the content 'which Khosrow said' (بگفت is a classical past form 'he said'); the object clause follows the verb as a reported-speech complement.
segments.s337-g30: که introduces the quoted/wished clause. با جان پاکش = 'with his pure soul' (پاکش is پاک + possessive -ش). خرد باد is an optative formula where باد is 'may it be' (optative), and جفت 'pair' functions predicatively: 'may wisdom be paired (with) his pure soul.' Word order is tersely poetic: predicate (خرد) + optative particle (باد) + complement (جفت).
segments.s337-g31: چو introduces a conditional/protasis meaning 'when/if'. آسوده باشیم is subjunctive/optative 'we be at ease' (باشیم is 1st-person-plural present-subjunctive used in protasis), and چیزی خوریم 'and eat something' uses خوریم in the same subjunctive/jussive mood to complete the condition.
segments.s337-g32: یک امشب is an adverbial phrase 'this very night / at least tonight'. از این چشمه specifies 'from/by this spring' and برنگذریم is a negative subjunctive/jussive first-person-plural 'let us not pass / we should not pass', formed classically as بر + negated verb; together it expresses the proposal not to pass the spring tonight.
segments.s337-g41: دریغ functions as a poetic interjection meaning “alas” or “pity” and introduces the lamenting phrase that follows. آن بلند اختر is a demonstrative + noun phrase (“that lofty/star”), where بلند is an epithet qualifying اختر. و رای او links another noun phrase (“and his counsel”); او is a possessive pronoun following the noun (standard Persian order: noun + pronoun). Word order here is poetic but straightforward: interjection → demonstrative + noun → conjunction + noun + possessive pronoun.
segments.s337-g42: This is a continuation/enumeration of qualities: بزرگی و دیدار و بالای او lists three nouns joined by و. او is the possessive pronoun that belongs to these attributes (explicit only on the last noun but understood for all). بالای is a poetic form meaning “high/superior” or “high station” and follows the same pattern (noun + possessive).
segments.s337-g43: خردمند is the subject “the wise (man/one).” از این کار is a prepositional phrase giving cause or object (“because of/by this deed”); از here functions roughly as “because of / on account of.” خندان is a predicative adjective “smiling, pleased,” and شود is the classical third‑person singular of شدن used to mean “becomes / will be” (here conveying result/prediction: “will be glad”). Word order (subject – prepositional phrase – predicate) is typical of older poetic Persian.
segments.s337-g44: که introduces the subordinate clause giving the reason/result (“that / because”). زنده کسی is an indefinite noun phrase “someone alive” (زنده = alive). پیش یزدان is a locative phrase “in the presence of God” (پیش = “in the presence of” here). شود again is the auxiliary/lexical verb “becomes / may be” (classical optative/indicative sense: “may be / will be”). The clause as a whole says why the wise rejoice: that someone may come alive/before God.
segments.s337-g45: که here opens a relative/interrogative “who” or “that who.” داند is the verb “knows” (third singular present). به گیتی is a circumstantial phrase “in the world” (به + گیتی = “in the world”); note that به is used as a locative/instrumental preposition. The embedded clause که او را چه بود uses او را (object marked pronoun) + چه بود (“what was”), i.e. “what happened to him / what he had.” The clause pattern is: relative particle + verb + locative phrase + subordinate content clause with object marker.
segments.s337-g46: چه گوییم is literally “what shall we say?” where گوییم is the first‑person plural present/subjunctive form (used here as a deliberative/optative: “what can/we should say”). و simply links the next phrase. گوش که یارد شنود is a relative/clausal question “is there an ear which the companion hears?” (گوش = “ear”; که introduces the relative clause; یارد = “companion/beloved” as agent; شنود is classical 3rd‑singular present/subjunctive of شنیدن “to hear”). In poetry the word order may be inverted for emphasis, but the structure is “what we might say, and is there an ear that a companion might hear with?”
segments.s337-g47: بدان here is the archaic/poetic prepositional phrase “to those” (in the manuscript split as ب + دان but functioning together as بدَان/بدان = “to those”); نامداران is the indirect object “the renowned ones.” چنین گفت گیو literally “thus said Giv”: such arrangement (indirect object first, then adverbial/quote marker چنین, then گفت, then the speaker’s name گیو) is typical of epic/poetic inversion — overall: “Giv said thus to those renowned men.”
segments.s337-g48: که introduces the reported content (“that …”). هرگز چنین is “never such / never thus.” نشنود is the classical negative present/subjunctive (ن + شنود) meaning “does not/he will not hear” or optatively “may not hear” — here translated “will never hear.” گوش نیو is a genitival phrase “the ear of the ney (reed‑flute)” where the possessor follows the possessed (noun + possessor), a frequent poetic word order. The line says: “that the ear of the ney will never hear such a thing.”
segments.s337-g17: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. از کوه is a prepositional phrase 'from/out of the mountain' modifying the verb. خورشید is the subject; سر برکشید is a past tense verb phrase (سر + برکشید) literally 'raised its head' — a poetic expression for the sun rising. Word order is somewhat inverted (temporal marker + locative + subject + verb) but follows classical Persian poetic style.
segments.s337-g18: ز چشم مهان is an ablative/genitive phrase 'from the eyes/of the sight of the guests' placed before the subject for emphasis. شاه is the subject and شد ناپدید is a two-part predicate where شد functions as a copular/inchoative 'became' and ناپدید is the adjectival/predicative 'unseen/vanished' — together 'became unseen' = 'vanished'.
segments.s337-g19: ببودند is an archaic/past verbal form (compound of ب + بودند) meaning 'there were' / 'they were'. ز آن جایگه is a locative 'from that place/at that spot'. شاه‌جوی is the noun 'aspirants to kingship' and functions as the logical subject, placed after the verb for poetic word order.
segments.s337-g20: به ریگ بیابان is a prepositional phrase 'toward the sandy desert' (به = toward/into; ریگ بیابان = sand of the desert). نهادند is the past tense 3rd plural of نهادن 'they placed/put/turned', here causatively used with روی 'face' to mean 'they turned/set their faces'. The object روی ('face') is literal/metaphorical: 'they faced toward the desert.'
segments.s337-g21: ز خسرو here means 'of the king' / 'concerning the king' and functions as the topic. ندیدند is the negative past 'they did not see'. جایی نشان is the object 'any sign / any trace' (جایی in negative contexts = 'in any place/anywhere'); together: 'they saw no sign of the king.'
segments.s337-g22: ز ره بازگشتند is a complete clause 'they returned from the road' (ز = from; ره = way/road; بازگشتند = returned). چون biهشان is an adverbial phrase: چون = 'as' / 'just as' introducing their state, and بیهشان (بیه + شان) is an archaic construction meaning 'without them / bereft' — together 'they turned back from the road, as they were bereft.'
segments.s337-g23: همه is the subject 'all / everyone'. تنگ‌دل گشته is a compound predication: تنگ‌دل 'narrow‑hearted, anguished' + گشته (perfect participle of شدن) 'have become' = 'had become sorrowful/narrow‑hearted'. و تافته links a second predicate: تافته (here adjectival) 'set apart / separated' — both describe the group's changed states.
segments.s337-g24: سپرده زمین is a participial phrase using سپرده (past participle of سپردن 'to entrust/hand over') with زمین 'the earth' — idiomatically 'handed over to the earth' = 'buried'. شاه نایافته places شاه (the king) after it with نایافته ('not found') describing him. The line compactly says the king had not been found and therefore had not been given to the earth (i.e. not buried); classical poetic ellipsis allows this compressed structure.
segments.s337-g1: چو is a temporal conjunction here ('when'). بهری is used poetically as 'a gleam/beam' (derived from بهر) and functions as the thing that appears; the listed senses for this lemma don't include 'gleam', so this is an archaic/poetic nominal use. ز تیره شب = 'from the dark night' (ز = 'from', تیره شب = 'dark night'), اندر is the locative 'into/in', and چمید is the verb meaning 'appeared/gleamed' in past tense; the verb comes at the end as typical in Persian poetry.
segments.s337-g2: کی here functions temporally ('when'/'then') introducing the clause about the famous person; نامور is the adjective 'renowned' used as the subject; پیش چشمه = 'before/at the spring' with پیش used spatially; رسید is the simple past 'reached/arrived' and appears at the end.
segments.s337-g3: بر آن آب روشن = 'upon that clear/bright water' (بر = on/upon, آن = that, آب روشن = clear water). سر و تن is a coordinated object 'head and body'. بشست is past tense third singular 'he washed'. The verb completes the action after its objects, following standard Persian order in narrative.
segments.s337-g4: همی is a continuous/adverbial particle ('kept on/continually'); خواند is here a past tense verb meaning 'he recited/sang' (poetic recitation); اندر نهان = 'in secret' (اندر = in/within, نهان = hidden/secretly). زند is uncertain in the provided sense list (likely an archaic form or part of a compound here) and thus treated as exceptional; و است is the copula 'and is/was' or a following linking element.
segments.s337-g5: چنین = 'thus/so'. گفت is the past tense of 'to say' ('he said'); the provided senses for گفت list nouns (conversation), so the verb sense is absent there and treated as exceptional. با + نامور + بخردان = 'to/with the famed and the wise' (بخردان = 'the wise', a plural or vocative form).
segments.s337-g6: که introduces the content/purpose clause ('that'/'so that'); باشید is an optative/subjunctive 'may you be' or second-person plural present used as a blessing ('be you'); پدرود = 'greeting/farewell/blessing' used as the wished state; تا جاودان = 'until eternal' = 'forever' (تا functioning as 'until' here). The sequence expresses a wish: 'may you remain greeted forever.'
segments.s337-g7: کنون = 'now'. چون here functions as a temporal conjunction ('when'/'as') introducing the ensuing event. برآرد is a present/habitual or narrative-present form 'raises / brings up'; سنان آفتاب is a poetic kenning 'the sun's spear' (i.e., a ray of sunlight). The verb is third-person singular.
segments.s337-g8: مبینید is second-person plural present ('you see' or used prophetically 'you will see'); دیگر here functions adverbially 'no more / no longer' in context (the listed senses give 'other/else', so it is used idiomatically). م and را are a split of the object 'مرا' ('me') — را is the definite object marker; the isolated م in the provided segmentation doesn't match the senses and is treated as exceptional. جز به خواب = 'except in a dream' (جز = except, به = 'into/in', خواب = 'dream').

sentences.s337-s25: For manliness and generosity, for justice and excellence, for bearing and high rank, and for glory and jewels.
sentences.s337-s26: In battle an elephant within the ranks was with the host; in the feast a beauty within was with a (decorative) cap.
sentences.s337-s5: Go back from this dry sand; do not remain here even if a musk-scented cloud should rain.
sentences.s337-s6: A violent wind comes out of the mountain; where will it not break the tree's boughs and leaves?
sentences.s337-s7: Much snow will fall from the black cloud; you will not find a way toward Iran.
sentences.s337-s8: At that speech the chiefs' spirits grew heavy; the Kandavarans lay down, stricken with pain.
sentences.s337-s17: The ground is warm and soft and the air is clear; in such fatigue it is not fitting to go.
sentences.s337-s18: They all descended at that spring at once, and they told Khosrow many tales.
sentences.s337-s19: for no one had seen such a marvel, and if one should remain long in the age...
sentences.s337-s20: We have never seen a king go like this, nor have we heard (of it) from the rebels.
sentences.s337-s13: They surged back to that spring, coming full of heart-sorrow and with burning anguish.
sentences.s337-s14: Upon that water, whoever came down would offer greeting to the king of the world.
sentences.s337-s15: Fariburz said what Khosrow had said: may wisdom be the pair of his pure soul.
sentences.s337-s16: If we are at ease and eat something, let us not pass by this spring tonight.
sentences.s337-s21: Alas for that lofty star and his counsel, his greatness, his appearance, and his high station.
sentences.s337-s22: The wise will smile at this deed, for someone may be made alive before God.
sentences.s337-s23: Who in the world can know what befell him, what we might say, and whether there is an ear his companion will hear with?
sentences.s337-s24: To those renowned men Giv said thus: ‘Never will the ear of the ney hear such a thing.’
sentences.s337-s9: When the sun lifted its head from the mountain, the king vanished from the sight of the guests.
sentences.s337-s10: From that spot there were aspirants to the throne; they turned their faces toward the sandy desert.
sentences.s337-s11: They saw no trace of the king; they turned back from the road, as they were bereft (of him).
sentences.s337-s12: All had become narrow‑hearted and set apart; the king had not been found nor laid to the earth.
sentences.s337-s1: When a gleam from the dark night shone forth, the famed one reached the spring.
sentences.s337-s2: Upon that clear water he washed his head and body; he kept reciting in secret.
sentences.s337-s3: Thus he spoke to the renowned and the wise, saying: 'May you be greeted forever.'
sentences.s337-s4: Now, when the sun raises its spear, you will see me no longer except in a dream.
